coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of novel lanthanum nanoparticles-graphene quantum dots coupled with zeolitic imidazolate framework and its electrochemical sensing application towards vitamin D3 deficiency
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Anusha, Tummala
Bhavani, Kalli Sai
Shanmukha Kumar, J.V.
Brahman, Pradeep Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) is an essential fat-soluble vitamin for healthy bone and muscle. Vitamin D deficiency is often not diagnosed as symptoms do not appear for a long time leading to cause osteoporosis, diabetes, and cancer. Currently, the testing of vitamin D is performed in sophisticated large-scale laboratories which are expensive and time-consuming for results. The development of a low-cost assay method could be helpful to detect vitamin D in limited-resource settings. Herein, for the first time, we report a novel electrochemical nanosensor for the analysis of vitamin D3 in biological fluids based on the lanthanum nanoparticles-graphene quantum dots (LaNPs-GQDs) coupled with zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface. For the construction of the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 sensing platform we have prepared low toxic graphene quantum dots (GQDs), lanthanum nanoparticles functionalized graphene quantum dots (LaNPs-GQDs), and zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIF-8). The results revealed that the combined impact of all the prepared materials enhanced the catalytic activity of the sensor by increasing the surface area of the sensor, the electron transfer rate between the electrode and vitamin D3 interface, and the facile oxidation of vitamin D3. The morphological and structural characteristics of the prepared LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite were investigated by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis spectroscopy (UV–vis), Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray diffraction technique (XRD). Moreover, the electrochemical properties of the nanocomposite were examined by cyclic voltammetry, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, chronoamperometry, and square wave voltammetry. Electrochemical studies revealed that an as-prepared sensor exhibited excellent electrochemical response towards vitamin D3 oxidation over a wide linear range from 0.00625 μM to 1.25 μM with an acceptable detection limit of 0.00610μM respectively. Besides, the proposed sensing platform showed an excellent anti-interference nature, reproducibility, acceptable recovery, and reliable stability. Finally, the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8@GCE sensor was successfully applied for the analysis of vitamin D3 in real specimens to show its clinical applicability.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pictorial representation of fabrication of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8@GCE.

A) FT-IR spectra of GQDs (I), LaNPs-GQDs (II), ZIF-8 (III) and LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 (IV); B) UV–vis spectra of GQDs (I), LaNPs-GQDs (II), ZIF-8 (III) and LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 (IV); C) XRD pattern of GQDs (inset Fig) (I), LaNPs-GQDs (II), ZIF-8 (III) and LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 (IV); D) XRD pattern of LaNPs-GQDs.
A) FESEM image of LaNPs-GQDs; B) FE-SEM image of ZIF-8; C) FE-SEM image of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8; D) EDX elemental mapping on LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8.
A) EDS spectrum of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 and EDS elemental mapping images of C (B), N (C), O (D), La (E) and Zn (F).

XPS spectra of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites: (A) XPS survey spectrum, (B) binding energy spectrum for C 1s, (C) Binding energy spectrum for N 1s, (D) Binding energy spectrum for O1s, (E) Binding energy spectrum for La 3d and (F) Binding energy spectrum for Zn 2p.

A) Nyquist plot of GCE (a), GQDs/GCE (b), LaNPs-GQDs/GCE @GCE (c), ZIF-8/GCE (d) and LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE (e) in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−; B) CV responses of GCE (a), GQDs/GCE (b), LaNPs-GQDs/GCE (c), ZIF-8/GCE (d) and LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE (e) in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at 30 mVs-1 scan rate; C) CVs at different scan rate (20-200 mVs-1); D) Relationship between peak current and logarithm of scan rate.

A) Electrochemical responses of GCE (I), GQDs@GCE (II), LaNPs-GQDs/GCE (III), ZIF-8 (IV) and LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE (V) in 0.05μM vitamin D3 (supporting electrolyte LiClO4, scan rate 30 mVs−1); B) CVs at different scan rate (20-200mVs−1) (Inset: Plot of log ʋ vs log Ip); C) Square wave voltammograms of vitamin D3 at different concentrations (0.00625-1.25μM) using LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE; F) Relationship between peak current and concentrations.

A) CV responses of La-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE composition; B) Relationship between La-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE composition vs peak current; C) CV responses of amount of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8; D) Relationship between amount of La-GQDs/ZIF-8 vs peak current; E) Chronoamperograms obtained for different concentration of vitamin d3 in 0.1 M LIClO4 at LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8@GCE ; Inset: Plots showing current, I vs t−1/2; F) Plot of slope of straight lines vs vitamin d3 concentration.

A) Inter-assay: electrochemical signal change response of 0.05μM vitamin D3 at 10 different sensors; B) Intra-assay: voltammetric response at a same sensor repetitively for 10 times at a certain concentration of vitamin D3 (0.05μM); C) The time stability study of the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE sensor for detection of vitamin D3; D) Influence of various interfering agents on the electrochemical response of vitamin D3; E) SWVs of vitamin D3 in serum sample at different concentrations (0.01μM-0.125μM); F) Calibration curve between concentration vs peak current; G) SWVs of vitamin D3 in urine sample at different concentrations (0.01μM-0.125μM); H) Calibration curve between concentration vs peak current.
Comparison of the proposed sensor with other reported electrodes/sensors.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of novel lanthanum nanoparticles-graphene quantum dots coupled with zeolitic imidazolate framework and its electrochemical sensing application towards vitamin D3 deficiency

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroanalytical Lab, Department of Chemistry, Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation, Vaddeswaram, Guntur, 522502, Andhra Pradesh, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electroanalytical Lab, Department of Chemistry, Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation, Vaddeswaram, Guntur-522502, Andhra Pradesh, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) is an essential fat-soluble vitamin for healthy bone and muscle. Vitamin D deficiency is often not diagnosed as symptoms do not appear for a long time leading to cause osteoporosis, diabetes, and cancer. Currently, the testing of vitamin D is performed in sophisticated large-scale laboratories which are expensive and time-consuming for results. The development of a low-cost assay method could be helpful to detect vitamin D in limited-resource settings. Herein, for the first time, we report a novel electrochemical nanosensor for the analysis of vitamin D3 in biological fluids based on the lanthanum nanoparticles-graphene quantum dots (LaNPs-GQDs) coupled with zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface. For the construction of the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 sensing platform we have prepared low toxic graphene quantum dots (GQDs), lanthanum nanoparticles functionalized graphene quantum dots (LaNPs-GQDs), and zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIF-8). The results revealed that the combined impact of all the prepared materials enhanced the catalytic activity of the sensor by increasing the surface area of the sensor, the electron transfer rate between the electrode and vitamin D3 interface, and the facile oxidation of vitamin D3. The morphological and structural characteristics of the prepared LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite were investigated by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis spectroscopy (UV–vis), Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray diffraction technique (XRD). Moreover, the electrochemical properties of the nanocomposite were examined by cyclic voltammetry, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, chronoamperometry, and square wave voltammetry. Electrochemical studies revealed that an as-prepared sensor exhibited excellent electrochemical response towards vitamin D3 oxidation over a wide linear range from 0.00625 μM to 1.25 μM with an acceptable detection limit of 0.00610μM respectively. Besides, the proposed sensing platform showed an excellent anti-interference nature, reproducibility, acceptable recovery, and reliable stability. Finally, the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8@GCE sensor was successfully applied for the analysis of vitamin D3 in real specimens to show its clinical applicability.

ce:section.ce:para: The antirachitic properties of vitamin D belong to the sub-division of the steroid group named ‘’secosteroid’’ which consists of three intact steroid rings [1]. Vitamin D significantly regulates calcium and phosphate homeostasis in the gut for the advancement of bone mineralization as well as the modulation of innate and adaptive immunity for improving the response to infections by organisms and forestalling autoimmune diseases, for example, various sclerosis [2]. The endocrine and autocrine functions of vitamin D inhibit the cell expansion, advancement of cell separation, and apoptosis [3]. Vitamin D occurs in two physiological significant forms, one is vitamin D2 (ergocalciferol) and another one is vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) [4]. Some evidence indicates that vitamin D3 is mainly responsible for 25(OH)D levels in serum. The precursor 7-dehydrocholesterol in the human skin absorbs the solar UV-β radiations and converts into pre-vitamin D3. To acquire the biologically active forms, vitamin D3 further hydrolyzes into two steps. In the initial step, vitamin D3 produces 25-hydroxy vitamin D3 through hydrolysis at C-25 position. 25(OH)D3 further undergoes hydroxylation at C-1 position to form 1,25 dihydroxy-vitamin D3 [5,6]. Vitamin D deficiency, inadequacy, and adequacy are characterized by the serum 25(OH)D cut-off levels. Based on clinical investigations, the sufficient level of vitamin D in the body is 70−250 nM while the deficiency level of vitamin D in the body is <50 nM [7,8]. Vitamin D deficiency is a significant worldwide health issue in all age groups. The depletion level of vitamin D can prompt various diseases such as cancer, cardiovascular disease, hypertension, depression, nonskeletal chronic diseases, lethal effects, osteomalacia in adults and asthma and rickets in children. However, the excess of vitamin D through diet could cause hypercalcemia [9,10]. Consequently, sensitive and selective assurance of the vitamin D3 level is extremely important for the early diagnosis and contravention of these diseases for the prosperity of human wellbeing. So far, researchers have proposed several classical methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), radio immune assay (RIA), liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LCMS/MS), and fluorescent techniques for the monitoring of vitamin D3 in food, biological fluids, and pharmaceutical products. These methods have some conspicuous points of interest, such as high affectability, selectivity, and precision. However, these are additionally associated with certain limitations such as long pre-concentration steps, professional operations, laborious, and require expensive instrumentations [11,12]. Hence, for the rapid, sensitive, simple, and low-cost identification of vitamin D deficiency, new sensing methodologies and point of care devices are urgently needed. Electrochemical techniques are an excellent alternative to classical methods and have various potential preferences, for example, fast response, low analysis time, inexpensive, low reagent utilization, suitable for miniaturization, high signal to noise proportion, and specific assurance of electro-active analytes [13].

Literature reveals that very few electrochemical sensors/methods have been developed by researchers for the monitoring of the accurate level of vitamin D3 in human serum. Various types of unmodified and modified electrodes such as rotating glassy carbon electrode [14], bare GCE [15,16], SiO2/GO/Ni(OH)2 modified GCE [17], Au-Pd/GCE [18], MIP-modified SPCE surface [19], LaNiO3-Nanoxide/GCE [20], boron-doped diamond electrode [21] and MWCNT/GCE [22] have been used to detect the vitamin D3 in pharmaceutical tablets only. These electrodes/methods showed some good results but high sensitivity, lower detection limit, good stability, excellent linear concentration range, low-cost electrode materials, applicability in serum samples within the deficiency range, etc. are such issues that need be addressed. Thus, the development of a reliable electrochemical sensor for assaying the vitamin D3 in biological samples has been proposed in the present work.

In recent years, an expanding number of materials such as polymers, metal nanoparticles, carbon nanotubes, graphene-based nanomaterials, etc. have been developed to modify electrodes for the analysis of various analytes. Especially, various forms of graphene nanomaterials such as graphene oxide, graphene nanoribbons, and graphene quantum dots have pulled remarkable interest in material science research. Acting as one of “rising star” carbon nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots exhibit superior performance due to intrinsic quality, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, excellent conductivity, large surface area, strong fluorescence, high stability, easy preparation, environmental friendliness. Due to their unique properties, GQDs have been considered excellent material for the development of electrochemical sensors with unique features such as stability, sensitivity, and selectivity [23,24]. GQDs possess a graphene structure inside the dots, regardless of the dot size, which endows them with some of the abnormal properties of graphene. GQDs can be prepared by two methods namely the top-down approach and bottom-up approach. In the proposed work, the bottom-up approach has been used for the synthesis of GQDs due to its several advantages such as controlling size and shape [25–29]. The development of hybrids of GQDs with various materials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, metal oxides, and metal nanoparticles have drawn the attention of researchers to use these materials in a variety of applications such as sensing, energy, etc. [30–35]. Among all materials, metal nanoparticles played a vital role in electrochemical sensing and catalysis. A typical issue in using metal nanoparticles (MNPs) for sensing and catalysis is the irreversible aggregation which decreases active surface sites and subsequently suppressed the catalytic performances. To address this issue, it is essential to develop an efficient route to retain active surface sites and monodispersity. For avoiding the aggregation of metal nanoparticles, oxygen-containing functionalities, such as carboxylic, carbonyl, and phenolic groups containing carbon supports are required. Of several carbon polymorphs, zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots are increasingly favorable for improving the electro-catalytic performance of MNPs because of their excellent conductivity, large specific surface area, and high charged carrier mobility. GQDs are ultra-small particles with oxygen-containing functional groups that can serve highly active sites to bind with other materials. Different and highly active sites contain zero-dimensional carbon materials are a more suitable platform for the coordination of various metal nanoparticles. The close interference of two distinct classes of materials for example GQDs and metal nanoparticles display novel functions such as structural changes in the carbon supports, very clean catalytic surface, high synergist ability, high electric conductivity, boosting electron transport effectiveness of metal nanoparticles. GQDs are also demonstrated to be excellent reducing and capping agents for the fabrication of various metal nanoparticles/GQDs hybrids [36–39]. To date, various metal nanoparticles such as Au, Pd, Pt, and Ag have been used for the preparation of metal nanoparticles/GQDs hybrids. In the proposed work, we chose lanthanum as metal nanoparticles for the preparation of LaNPs-GQDs hybrids. Rare earth metals including lanthanum have been considered broadly because of their extraordinary catalytic feature in redox reactions, fast oxygen ion mobility, and excellent adaptability in catalytic reactions, which will be a stimulus to improve the sensor performance. Besides, lanthanum exhibits a more noteworthy ionic radius than the other elements affecting numerous structural properties and making it suitable for GQDs hybrids [40–42].

Recently metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have received extraordinary consideration in the electrochemical sensor field because MOFs exhibit remarkable properties such as incredibly high specific surface areas, stable chemical properties, flexible pore sizes and functionalities, explicit micropore volumes, tunable structure, and rich electrochemistry/spectroscopy. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are the assembly of various metal ions (such as transition metals, p-block elements, alkaline earth metals, and actinides) and tunable organic linkers (carboxylates, amines, nitrates, phosphates, sulfonates, etc.). Zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIFs) is a tetrahedrally-coordinated transition metal particles (for example Fe, Co, Cu, Zn) associated by imidazolate or its derivatives linkers. Contrast to different MOFs, ZIFs show excellent properties such as higher thermal, hydrothermal, chemical stability, very less cytotoxicity, hydrophobic properties, water dependability, and simple synthetic procedure. ZIFs have broad application prospects in energy storage, gas separation, chemical sensing, drug delivery, and catalysis. Among all ZIFs, Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIF-8) have attracted more consideration because ZIF-8 provides a well-characterized pore structure, high thermal, and chemical stability [43–47]. In the recent past, the use of ZIF-8 in sensor fabrication has been increased but its low conductivity inhibits the electron transfer between the sensor surface and analyte interface. However, the conductivity of ZIF-8 could be significantly increased by embedding different nanomaterials including metal nanomaterials and carbon nanomaterials [48,49].

In the present work, we propose the simple synthetic route to prepare La-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite based on lanthanum nanoparticle functionalized graphene quantum dots encapsulated with ZIF-8. The synthesized material was carefully characterized by X-ray diﬀ ;raction (XRD), UV–vis spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The electrochemical sensor was fabricated by immobilizing the proposed material on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The electrochemical properties of La-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE were explored using cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and square wave voltammetry. The results showed that the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 could be a highly effective material for the detection of vitamin D3. The developed sensor displayed unique features such as high sensitivity and selectivity, improved electro-kinetics, lower detection limit, a broad linear range, high stability, and reproducibility to detect vitamin D3 in biological samples. The proposed sensor showed reliable results in real samples as proved by the recovery experiments. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report on the use of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite for the fabrication of electrochemical sensors and use in the determination of vitamin D3 in human serum and urine samples.

All reagents and solvents were of the highest purity and were used without further purification. The citric acid (CA), lanthanum nitrate, zinc nitrate hexahydrate, 2-methyl imidazole, hydrochloric acid, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), were supplied by Himedia Pvt.Ltd. India. Other reagents including sodium hydroxide, potassium chloride, potassium ferricyanide, potassium ferrocyanide, sulfuric acid, cholecalciferol (97–102 %), lithium perchlorate (99 %), ethanol, hydrochloric acid were procured from Himedia Pvt. Ltd. India. By dissolving a suitable amount of vitamin D3 in ethanol and diluting the mixture to 50 ml with ethanol using a 50 ml volumetric flask, a vitamin D3 solution of 0.001 M was prepared. The stock solution was maintained in the dark space of the refrigerator. 0.1 M LiClO4 was used as the supporting electrolyte during all experiments. NaOH was used to adjust the pH. All the stock solutions were prepared freshly at the time of experiments.

The UV-absorption spectra (200−800 nm) of the as-synthesized materials were investigated by using UV–vis-NIR Spectrophotometer, V-670, Jasco. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of the prepared samples were recorded in the range 4000–500 cm−1 with a CDTL-LAB4-EQP-006 spectrophotometer. The phase structure and crystallinity of the as-prepared materials were measured by X-ray diﬀ ;raction (XRD) studies in the range from 10−90°. XRD experiments were carried out using Rigaku miniflex 600 X-ray automatic diffractometer from Rigaku Corporation, Japan. The surface morphology of the as-synthesized products was examined by ﬁeld emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Carl Ziess Model: Neon-40). The elemental composition of the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 material was performed by using EDX, Carl Ziess Model: Neon-40. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were carried out using a PHI 5000 VersaProbe III (Physical Electronics).

Electrochemical experiments were conducted on the Metrohm PGSTAT-204 instrument connected with NOVA software. A conventional three-electrode setup was used, which consists of an Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) as a reference electrode, Pt wire as the auxiliary electrode, and LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE as the working electrode. The pH of the solutions was studied by using Systronic-362μ pH meter. Electrochemical methods including cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), chronoamperometry (CA), and square wave voltammetry (SWV) were used to study the electrochemical behavior of GQDs, LaNPs-GQDs, ZIF-8 and LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 modified GCE electrodes for the determination of vitamin D3 under ambient conditions. EIS measurements were performed in 5 mM Fe (CN)6 3−/4− in 0.1 M KCl at open circuit potential in the frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 105Hz with the potential amplitude of 5 mV. CV was carried out in 0.1 M LiClO4 in the presence of 0.05μM vitamin D3 in the potential range from 0.2 V to 1.2 V at a scan rate of 30 mVs−1. SWV measurements were recorded in 0.1 M LiClO4 containing 0.05μM vitamin D3 with a frequency of 25 Hz at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 in the potential range from 0.5 V to 1.3 V. All working solutions were purged with nitrogen for 30 min before measurements. All the electrochemical experiments were carried out at 25 ± 1 °C.

A simple bottom-up approach has been employed to synthesize GQDs as reported in the literature [23]. Single-step pyrolysis of citric acid was used to produce GQDs followed by dispersion of carbonized products into an alkaline solution. In a typical procedure, 2.0gms citric acid was heated to 200 °C in a beaker on the heating mantle and converted into pale yellow within a 5 min and about 25−30 min later turned into orange color confirming the formation of GQDs. The melted dense solution of citric acid was dropwise added into 50 ml of 10 mg/mL NaOH solution at constant stirring. The resultant solution was then neutralized with NaOH. The GQDs aqueous solution was stored in a refrigerator.

The LaNPs-GQDs nanocomposite was prepared through a co-precipitation method using a reported method with slight modification [50]. Initially, 20 mL of the freshly prepared GQDs solution was taken into a beaker, and 10 mL of 0.5 M La(NO3)2.6H2O solution was added to GQDs solution in inert condition at constant stirring and then 10 mL of NaOH solution was added dropwise. This mixture solution was kept at 80 °C for about 1 h to form homogeneous precipitation. Finally, the mixture was cool down to room temperature and the pale-yellow precipitate was collected using centrifugation. The precipitate was washed 3–4 times with deionized water, and vacuum dried at 70 °C for 24 h to obtain the LaNPs-GQDs nanocomposite.

A routine wet method was selected and applied for the preparation of ZIF-8 outlined in the previous report [51]. For the synthesis of ZIF-8, 0.22gm (20 mmol) of zinc nitrate hexahydrate was dispersed in 40 mL of deionized water and 4.54gm of 2-methyl imidazole was dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water. Both the solutions were mixed and stirred vigorously for 2 h at room temperature and then the product was separated through centrifugation and washed 3–4 times with deionized water. Finally, the obtained white color solid was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 h. Schematic representation LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 formation is given in Scheme 1 .

In the present study, a glassy carbon electrode with a working surface area of 0.196 cm2 (5 mm) was utilized for the fabrication of the sensor. 2.0 mg of LaNPs-GQDs and 1 mg of ZIF-8 (an optimal mass ratio of 2:1 for LaNPs-GQDs to ZIF-8) were dispersed in a mixed solution of de-ionized water and N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), followed by ultrasonic agitation for 30 min to achieve a well-dispersed suspension. Before modification, the bare GCE was elaborately cleaned by polishing with 0.1, 0.3, and 0.05 μm alumina slurries sequentially on a polishing cloth to obtain a mirror-like surface, and then the electrode was sonicated in absolute ethanol and distilled water respectively. The cleaned electrode was dried under ambient conditions. Then, 10 μL of La-GQDs@ZIF-8 suspension was uniformly coated on bare GCE and dried in air for approximately 3 h to get a uniform layer of La-GQDs@ZIF-8. A similar procedure was used to fabricate the GQDs modified GCE (GQDs/GCE), LaNPs-GQDs modified GCE (LaNPs-GQDs/GCE and ZIF-8 modified GCE (ZIF-8/GCE) as control electrodes for comparison. A pictorial depiction of sensor fabrication and electrochemical signaling is given in Scheme 2 .

The standard addition method was used for accurate determination of vitamin D3 in human serum and urine samples. All experiments were performed in compliance with relevant ethical guidelines. To prepare the blood serum samples, initially, the serum sample was collected from the local pathology lab and transferred into micro tubes and stored at 4−8 °C until analysis. Generally, human serum samples contain inorganic components, proteins, electrolytes, hormones, and exogenous substances. Proteins were separated by adding 3.0 mL methanol as a serum protein sedimentation agent in 1.5 mL serum. The mixture was vortexed for 2 min and then centrifuged for 15 min at 3500 rpm. A protein-free human serum was obtained by filtering the supernatant layer using a 0.45 μm filter and stored in the refrigerator. For vitamin D3 analysis, a 0.2 mL serum sample and 39.8 mL lithium perchlorate were transferred into a 40 mL voltammetric. Similarly, 10 mL of fresh urine sample was collected from a healthy volunteer and centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 20 min to remove the precipitated proteins. The supernatant was filtered using a 0.45 μm filter. For the detection of vitamin D3, 0.2 mL of a treated urine sample and 39.8 mL of LiClO4 was transferred into a 40 mL voltammetric cell. The diluted serum and urine samples were spiked with different amounts of vitamin D3 and studied using square wave voltammetry.

The different functional groups of GQDs, LaNPs-GQDs, ZIF-8, and LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites were studied using FT-IR (Fig. 1 A). The strong broad peak around 3390 cm−1 shows the OH bond stretching vibration confirms the presence of intercalated water molecules on the GQDs. The two peaks located at 1634 cm−1 and 1571 cm-1 are associated with CO and C=C stretching vibrations. A small peak obtained at 1469 cm-1 and 1380 cm-1 are related to the C–H and COH stretching vibration from residual citric acid respectively suggesting the incomplete carbonization of citric acid. These outcomes suggested that the GQDs surface contains various oxygen-containing functional groups and the better solubility of the GQDs in water. The FT-IR spectrum of LaNPs-GQDs demonstrates the characteristic peak at 3431 cm-1 which is at a higher wavelength as compared to GQDs (3390 cm-1) suggesting the absorbance of water by LaNPs-GQDs nanocomposite and the absorption peak appeared at 1634 cm-1 corresponds to the CO stretching vibrations. The other peak located at 593cm-1 is related to the La-O bond, suggesting the bonding of lanthanum nanoparticles to the COH group on GQDs surface. However, the peaks presented in the GQDs (1571 cm-1 and 1469 cm-1) were disappeared in the LaNPs-GQDs nanocomposite indicating the LaNPs were strongly bonded to the GQDs [52–54]. The FT-IR spectrum of the as-synthesized ZIF-8 is identical with spectrum available literature, strongly indicate the formation of ZIF-8. The strong absorption band generated at 3470 cm-1 was caused by the OH of H2O and the two absorption peaks observed at 3100 cm-1 and 2867 cm-1 suggest the presence of aromatic and the aliphatic C–H stretching vibration of the imidazole ligand. Meanwhile, the CC, C=N and =C-H stretching vibrations are located at 1650cm-1, 1510 cm-1 and 1093 cm-1 respectively. The characteristic peaks in the range from 1250−1500 cm−1 are associated with the imidazolium ring stretching. Additionally, the peak appeared at 444 cm-1 could be related to the Zn-N stretch mode. These results confirmed the Zn2+ and the 2-methylimidazole have formed the ZIF-8 nanocomposite [43,44,55,56]. In contrast, the absorption peaks of LaNPs-GQDs and ZIF-8 which could be observed on the spectrum of La-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoparticles, confirming the strong interaction between the LaNPs-GQDs and ZIF-8.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs, LaNPs-GQDs, ZIF-8, and LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 are presented in Fig. 1B. GQDs exhibited two broad bands at 216 nm and 355 nm which ascribed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains of GQDs and n-π* transition of the functional groups presents in the GQDs, implying the successful formation of GQDs [57]. When lanthanum nanoparticles were added to the GQDs, the two absorption bands of GQDs at 216 nm and 355 nm shifted to 213 nm and 283 nm. The enhancement in peak intensity and shifting in the peak position of LaNPs-GQDs confirms the strong interaction between the lanthanum nanoparticles and GQDs. Besides, the formation of the ZIF-8 was analyzed by the UV–Vis spectroscopic method in a solid phase. ZIF-8 displays two absorption bands at 220 nm and 285 nm which are in accordance with the literature [58,59]. In general, organic linkers in MOF structures play an important role in light absorption. The absorption band observed at 220 nm is related to the absorbance of the organic ligand in ZIF-8. Afterward, the encapsulation of LaNPs-GQDs into ZIF-8 shows two absorption bands at 214 nm and 267 nm, which ascribed to the absorption spectra of ZIF-8 and LaNPs-GQDs. Besides, peak intensity increased as compared to GQDs, LaNPs-GQDs, and ZIF-8. These findings clearly indicate the successful incorporation of LaNPs-GQDs into the framework of ZIF-8 and also negatively charged LaNPs-GQDs bound to ZIF-8 via electrostatic interactions.

The structural analysis of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite was further carried out by XRD. The crystalline phases of GQDs, LaNPs-GQDs, ZIF-8, and LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 were depicted in Fig. 1C. The XRD pattern of GQDs exhibited a wide diffraction peak located at around 2θ = 26.4° corresponding to (002) planes of the GQDs. The peaks are broad due to the small size of GQDs and the larger d-space (0.34 nm) of GQDs, indicates the presence of oxygenated functional groups on the surface. The XRD pattern of GQDs is identical to the XRD pattern of previous studies [60,61]. LaNPs-GQDs nanocomposite has two prominent peaks at 2θ values 27.2° and 43.84° respectively, indicating the polycrystalline nature of the composite. The diffraction peak located at 2θ = 27.2° is related to the GQDs. The increase in peak intensity and shift in peak position in LaNPs-GQDs as compared to GQDs, confirms the effect of metal nanoparticles (shown in Fig. 1D). The other diffraction peak located at 2θ = 43.84° (6 2 2) suggests the hexagonal structure of lanthanum nanoparticles [62,63]. These results confirm that the lanthanum nanoparticles were effectively incorporated on the surface of GQDs. On the other hand, strong and narrow peaks in the XRD pattern of ZIF-8 exhibits high crystalline nature [64–66]. The presence of strong peaks at 2θ = 7.44°, 10.42°, 12.74°, 14.78°, 16.48°, 18.04° correspond to the (110) (200) (211) (220) (310) (222) planes of the ZIF-8, suggesting that ZIF-8 has a good porous structure. In contrast, the diffraction peaks of ZIF-8 had disappeared in the spectrum of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoparticles, and overall intensity increases confirming the formation of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite.

The surface morphology of the prepared nanocomposites was investigated by FE-SEM. Fig. 2 A–C show the SEM images of the LaNPs-GQDs, ZIF-8, and LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites. The FE-SEM image of the LaNPs-GQDs (Fig. 2A) shows that lanthanum nanoparticles were successfully embedded into graphene quantum dots to form composite particles. Furthermore, particles are self-assembled and porous due to the interaction between GQDs and LaNPs. At this stage, it was very difficult to find out the appearance of the GQDs because of their transparency, and poor crystallinity. It is obvious that pure ZIF-8 crystals possess typical rhombic dodecahedral morphology as shown in Fig. 2B. The FE-SEM image of the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. 2C) reveals the uniform distribution of ZIF-8 nanocrystals on the La-GQDs surface. Furthermore, the EDX spectrum obtained for LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 is shown in Fig. 3 A. The observed EDX spectrum of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 showed the strong peaks for carbon (C), Nitrogen (N), Oxygen (O), Lanthanum (La), and Zinc (Zn), demonstrating the formation of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite. The data about the weight percentages obtained for C, N, O, La, and Zn is given in the inset of this spectrum. Besides, elemental mapping analysis of the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite was performed to investigate the elements present in it. In this investigation, at first, a specific SEM region was selected (Fig. 2D), and the respective elemental maps were obtained for each of the elements present. These maps are illustrated in Fig. 3(B–F). It can be seen that the C, N, O, La, and Zn are homogeneously distributed in the prepared nanocomposite.

XPS was performed to further investigate the surface elemental composition and interaction between active sites (ZIF-8) and LaNPs-GQDs in LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 composite. The wide scan XPS spectrum of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 is depicted in Fig. 4 A. The major peaks appearing at around 285, 498, 531, 836, and 1036.5eV were related to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, La 3d, and Zn 2p, respectively, which is in good agreement with the SEM-EDX spectrum and elemental mapping. The high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 4B) were deconvoluted into five distinct types of carbon peaks at 284.2, 285.9, 286.2, 287.5, and 288.5 eV, where all peaks shifted to high energy. These peaks are related to CC, C–O, C-N, C=O, and O=C-OH, respectively confirming the presence of oxygen functional groups and nitrogen in the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 framework. The N1 s spectrum is shown in Fig. 4C. The peaks at 398.1 eV, 398.6 eV, and 397.9 eV indexed to the N in 2-methylimidazole and pyridinic nitrogen respectively, indicating that ZIF-8 successfully incorporated into LaNPs-GQDs. Moreover, The pyridinic N and pyrrole N in ZIF-8 might improve the electrochemical activity of La-GQDs. The O1 s shows three peaks at 531.6, 531.2, and 530.3 eV corresponding to the O2− in the Zn–O bonding, carboxylate species, and CO groups respectively in ZIF-8 and GQDs and the lower binding energy component at 529.2 eV is ascribed to La-O bonds as shown in Fig. 4D. The core-level spectrum of La in Fig. 4E could be deconvoluted into two definite peaks with binding energies at 834.5 and 851.7 eV, which are related to the spin-orbital energy level of La 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively and their energy gap of 16 eV. Additionally, the other two peaks at 837.2 and 853.9 eV are La 3d satellite peaks which are attributed to the relocation of electrons of O-2p to an unoccupied La 5f indicating La+3 oxidation state [67]. The highly magnified view of the core level XPS spectrum of Zn 2p is shown in Fig. 4F, and it shows the obtained XPS peaks at binding energies of 1021.06 and 1044.38 eV related to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 respectively, and their spin energy differed by 23 eV, representing that Zn in the LaNPs-GQDs @ZIF-8 composite was of +2 oxidation state [68]. The elemental ratio of C, N, O, La, and Zn was quantified as 16.71 %, 26.10 %, 39.58 %, 461.20 %, and 166.22%, respectively.

To analyze the kinetics of the electron transfer of the proposed electrodes throughout the process of modiﬁcation, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) as a powerful technique was employed. The semicircle of the Nyquist plot at higher frequencies corresponds to the limited kinetics (Rct) and the linear line at lower frequencies relates to the diffusion-controlled reaction kinetics. Fig. 5 A shows the Nyquist plots of bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, LaNPs-GQDs/GCE, ZIF-8/GCE, and LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3–4– probe in the frequency range from 0.1−10 kHz. The bare GCE electrode exhibited a pretty large semicircle area showed relatively high Rct at the electrode surface. The modification of GCE with GQDs reduced the semicircle diameter revealing improved conductivity and surface area. In the case of LaNPs-GQDs/GCE, the diameter of the semi-circle was further reduced as compared to GQDs@GCE which is attributed to the presence of lanthanum nanoparticles. LaNPs increased the conductivity and catalytic activity of the electrode. At the ZIF-8@GCE, the semi-circle diameter was further decreased due to the pore structure of ZIF-8. However, when GCE was modified with LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite, the Rct was diminished remarkably. This was mainly due to the combined effect of LaNPs-GQDs and ZIF-8. This is evidence for the endorsement of high charge transfer kinetics and poor electron charge transfer resistance at the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite. These findings confirmed that the surface modification of electrode with LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 has been successfully conducted and LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE exhibited excellent catalytic activity.

To obtain further knowledge about the surface of modified electrodes, cyclic voltammetry was used. Sequential formation and electrochemical activity of the proposed sensor was evaluated by CV in 5.0 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− solution in 0.1 mol L–1 KCl between range −0.2 and 0.6 V potential window at 30 mV s−1 scan rate as shown in Fig. 5B. Bare GCE exhibited a very small redox peak suggesting slow electron transfer at the interface. GQDs modified GCE showed enhanced peak current. Further increment in the peak current was noticed at LaNPs-GQDs/GCE and ZIF-8/GCE as compared to the GQDs/GCE. This could be due to the excellent electrical conductivity of lanthanum nanoparticles and ZIF-8. LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE showed significant enhancement in the peak currents as compared to all electrodes due to its high surface area and good conductivity. Besides, GQDs contain abundant functional groups that possess rich exposed active sites, lanthanum nanoparticles exhibit good catalytic activity and ZIF-8 provides large surface area and porosity. These excellent properties contribute to the improvement of the sensitivity of the sensor.

The effect of varying sweep rate on the electrochemical behavior of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE was also studied. Fig. 5C clearly shows that all scan rates resulted in well-defined redox peaks and enhancement in both cathodic and anodic peak currents and peak potentials with a rise in scan rate. Anodic and cathodic peak currents showed a linear relation with the logarithm of the scan rate (Fig. 5D) yielding a regression equation Ipa (μA) = 264.33 (log (scan rate)) -223.12 (R2 = 0.9852) and Ipc (μA) = 119.08 (R2 = 0.9919). This affirmed that the electrode reaction was diﬀ ;usion controlled with quasi-reversible behavior. The fact indicated that the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 was effectively associated with the electrode for facile electron transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical sensing performance of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE towards vitamin D3
ce:section.ce:para:
Vitamin D3 can be electrochemically oxidized via two-proton coupled with the two-electron transfer reaction, which provides a single well-defined anodic peak at low potential indicating the occurrence of oxidation reaction: cholecalciferol into dihydroxy cholecalciferol and the irreversible oxidation reaction. To probe the electrochemical activities of GCE and modified GCEs towards vitamin D3 oxidation, cyclic voltammetry experiments were performed. The cyclic voltammetric measurements were performed in the anodic potential sweeping from 0.2 to 1.3 V in the 0.1 M lithium perchlorate as a supporting electrolyte containing 0.05μM vitamin D3 at a scan rate of 30 mVs−1(Fig. 6 A). Excellent results were obtained at LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE as compared to other electrodes. Well-defined peak, low oxidation potential, and enlarged peak current are due to good electric conductivity and more abundant electroactive sites facilitating smooth electron transfer at analyte and electrode interface. In order to evaluate whether the oxidation process on the electrode surface is diffusion or adsorption controlled, cyclic voltammetry at different scan rates (20-200mVs−1) was performed at LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE in 0.1 M LiClO4 containing 0.05μM vitamin D3 (Fig. 6B). A linear increment in peak current and peak potential with the scan rate revealed that the oxidation of vitamin D3 was a diffusion-controlled process. The graph of the log Ipa versus logϑ showed linearity and the obtained slope value is nearly equal to the theoretical value (inset Fig. 6B). This behavior also confirms the vitamin D3 oxidation process is diffusion-controlled and is expressed as follows.
log Ipa (μA) = 0.3615 log ϑ + 0.422 (R2 = 0.9856)

SWV technique is further used to evaluate the applicability of the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE sensor for the quantitative determination of vitamin D3. Fig. 6C shows square wave voltammograms in 0.1 M LiClO4 containing different concentrations of vitamin D3 at the proposed sensor. Oxidation peak current of vitamin D3 enhanced gradually with increasing concentrations ranging from 0.00625μM to 1.25μM. The calibration plot of oxidation peak current responses against vitamin D3 concentrations (Fig. 6D) has two linear segments. The first linear range was obtained from 0.00625−0.0625 μM with a linear regression equation of Ipa (μA) = 2.4x + 11.12 (R2 = 0.983) and the second linear range was 0.125–1.25 μM with a linear regression equation of Ipa (μA) = 4.05x + 15.415 (R2 = 0.995). As per signal to noise (S/N) = 3, the detection limits of vitamin D3 was found to be 0.00610μM. These "two linear ranges" may be induced by the quick formation of the oxidation products of vitamin D3. When vitamin D3 concentration was high, a large number of oxidation products formed on the electrode surface, which prevented the adsorption and oxidation of vitamin D3, thus the response sensitivity decreased. A lower detection limit and a wide linear concentration range were achieved at the proposed sensor as compared to previous reports making it much better suitable to detect vitamin D3 deficiency as per clinical guidelines. Increased electrical conductivity, large speciﬁc surface area, and ordered pore structure of LaNPs-GQDS@ZIF-8 facilitated ease in electron transfer and mass transfer leading to enhanced sensitivity of the sensor. Table 1 illustrates a comparison for the detection of vitamin D3 at LaNPs-GQDS@ZIF-8/GCE with other modiﬁed electrodes reported in the literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of nanocomposite composition and loading amount on GCE
ce:section.ce:para: It has been found that the composition of the modifier has a significant impact on the sensitivity and selectivity of the electrochemical response. The ratio of the LaNPs-GQDs to ZIF-8 in nanocomposite influenced the vitamin D3 oxidation at the electrode surface. To enhance the sensitivity of the sensor, four different compositions (1:1, 2:1, 3:1, and 5:1 (w/w)) of the LaNPs-GQDs to ZIF-8 were used for sensor fabrication. The electrochemical performance of each composition was studied in 0.1 M lithium perchlorate as supporting electrolyte containing 0.05 μM vitamin D3 by using the CV method as shown in Fig. 7 A. As can be seen in Fig. 7B, the oxidation peak current response of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE with mass ratio 2:1 was found to be higher than the others indicating that the mass ratio 2:1 showed faster electron transfer kinetics as compared to other ratios compositions. Finally, from the observations, we chose 2.0 mg of LaNPs-GQDs, and 1.0 mg of ZIF-8 as an optimum ratio for the fabrication of the sensor. The loading amount of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 over the electrode surface has been optimized to obtain a better electrode response (Fig. 7C). Various amount of dispersion solution from 2 to 16 μL was cast on the GCE and peak response was analyzed by recording cyclic voltammogram in 0.05μM vitamin D3 containing 0.1 M lithium perchlorate as supporting electrolyte. It was evident from Fig. 7D that the oxidation peak current of vitamin D3 increased up to 10 μL after that peak current started declining. This is due to the too thick LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 film probably decreased the conductivity and thick film also became unstable on the electrode surface. It was also observed that the oxidation peak potential of vitamin D3 was shifted to a more positive side with increasing the concentration of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8.Thus 10 μL was selected as an optimized amount of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 for sensor fabrication.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chronoamperometry study of Vitamin D3 at LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE
ce:section.ce:para:
The diffusion coefficient (D) for the electrochemical oxidation of vitamin D3 at LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE was studied by using chronoamperometric measurements and Cottrell equation. The chronoamperograms of different concentrations of vitamin D3 in 0.1 M lithium perchlorate (LiClO4) were recorded at a fixed potential of 1.1 V as shown in Fig. 7E. A linear curve was obtained from the chronoamperometric measurements for the various concentrations of vitamin D3 by plotting the graph between current versus t−1/2 (Inset Fig. 7E). This denotes that the transient current must be controlled by a diffusion-controlled process. Afterward, we plotted the slopes of the resulting straight lines against the concentrations of vitamin D3 standardized for chronoamperometric measurements (Fig. 7F). In the next stage, the final slope value was used to calculate the diffusion coefficient (D). The diffusion coefficient(D) of vitamin D3 at the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8@GCE was calculated using the Cottrell equation [69].
I = nFAD1/2Cπ−1/2 t−1/2
D= (m/nFACЛ1/2)2
Where D represents the diffusion coefficient, n represents the number of electrons (2), A represents the area of the electrode (0.0196 cm2), F represents Faraday’s constant (96,485 C/mol), C denotes the initial concentration(mol/cm3), π = 3.14, I represents the anodic peak current and t denotes time (0−300 s). By substituting all parameters in the Cottrell equation, the diffusion coefficient of vitamin D3 was found to be 2.56 × 10−4 cm2s-1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, repeatability and stability of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: For the validation of the proposed method/sensor, different parameters such as repeatability, reproducibility, stability analysis were studied. To investigate the reproducibility of the proposed sensor towards sensing of vitamin D3, ten LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE sensors were fabricated independently under the identical conditions and were successfully employed in the supporting electrolyte, serum, and urine samples containing 0.05μM vitamin D3 (Fig. 8 A). The results showed that the SWV signal was almost similar for the ten fabricated sensors. The method was found to be reproducible as the RSD values were less than 1.48 % indicated that the reproducibility of the proposed sensor is satisfactory. Besides, the repeatability of the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE sensor was investigated by the determination of 0.05μM vitamin D3 samples for ten successive replicates (Fig. 8B). The relative standard deviation was calculated to be 1.75 % demonstrating a high precision. The long-term stability of the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE sensor was assessed by storing the modified electrode at room temperature for 7 weeks and recording the corresponding peak current under the same concentration of vitamin D3 every week. After 7 weeks, the modified electrode still retained 98.3 % of the first recorded current as shown in Fig. 8C. The corresponding voltammograms are given in Fig.S1 in supplementary file. As mentioned in the introduction part, GQDs, LaNPs, and ZIF-8 enhance the rate of vitamin D3 oxidation and stability of the sensor. GQDs possess a large number of active sites to stabilize LaNPs and ZIF-8 material and therefore responsible for the excellent stability of the sensor. The above results revealed the excellent reproducibility, repeatability, and long-term stability of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of common interferents on sensor performance
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the proposed method/sensor is one of the most significant aspects to validate its practical applications. To confirm the selectivity for vitamin D3 detection, the effects of various interferences were investigated using SWV measurements. Co-existing surface-active compounds that may get adsorbed or oxidized on the electrode surface within the potential range of vitamin D3 oxidation considerably impacting peak current. As shown in Fig. 8D some common biological substances such as vitamins A, E, B2, B6, C, inorganic compounds like Na+, Cl−, Mg+2, and other organic compounds like cholesterol, uric acid, l-cysteine, and glucose were tested on LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE and followed by successive additions of vitamin D3. The corresponding voltammograms are given in Fig.S2 in supplementary file. It was clear that interfering agents had no critical influence on the detection of vitamin D3. However, vitamin D3 molecules gave a fast and significant increase in the current response after each addition of vitamin D3. Based on the above experimental results, the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE sensor shoed resistance to the possible interfering agents probing the excellent selectivity of vitamin D3 detection.

In order to investigate the practical applicability of the novel LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE sensor, vitamin D3 was analyzed from diluted human and urine samples in 0.1 M lithium perchlorate as supporting electrolyte by using the SWV technique via standard addition method. First of all, serum and urine samples were pre-treated as mentioned in the experimental section for the detection of vitamin D3. For this purpose, 0.2 ml pre-treated human serum sample was diluted with 39.8 ml lithium perchlorate. Firstly, the square wave response was recorded with the blank serum sample using LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE. The serum sample did give any significant current response. Thereafter, the known concentrations of vitamin D3 were spiked into diluted human serum samples, and SWVs were recorded. An enhancement of oxidation peak current was observed with the increasing concentrations of vitamin D3 as shown in the Fig. 8E. The serum sample was spiked with six different concentrations of vitamin D3 which showed a linear response with peak current (Fig. 8F). The recoveries of vitamin D3 in the human serum were calculated from Fig. 8E using standard addition method and tabulated in Table 2 . The calculated recoveries ranged from 97.94%–98.82%. The RSD values were in the range of 1.06–1.22 %. Excellent recoveries were obtained with the proposed sensor confirms that our proposed vitamin D3 sensor is precise and reliable. A similar experiment was performed with the urine sample as well. Blank urine samples did not show any significant peak indicating that absence of vitamin D3 (Fig. 8G). The urine sample was spiked with six different concentrations of vitamin D3 which showed a linear response with peak current (Fig. 8H). The recoveries of vitamin D3 in the human urine were calculated from Fig. 8G using the standard addition method and tabulated in Table 2. Acceptable recoveries were obtained in the range of 98.97–99.43 % with the designed sensor. The above experimental results demonstrated that the LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8/GCE is efficient and applicable for the sensing of vitamin D3 in real samples.

In this study, we have described the synthesis and application of LaNPs-GQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite in electrochemical nanosensor fabrication for vitamin D3 analysis in clinical samples at physiological pH. The introduction of ZIF-8 not only improved the electrochemical signal of vitamin D3 but also increased the GQDs synergistic activity. The morphological and structural characterization of the proposed sensor shows a strong interaction between the LaNPs-GQDs and ZIF-8 in the nanocomposite. The fabricated sensor was used as a precise, sensitive, and selective tool for squarewave voltammetric determination of vitamin D3 in biological samples. Interestingly, the proposed sensor displayed a wide linear range from 0.00625 μM to 1.25 μM with a limit of detection of 0.00610μM. In addition, the single surface of the fabricated sensor can be used for multiple determinations over several weeks because there is no leaching out of the electrode material from the sensor surface indicating its long-term stability. The developed sensor shows great opportunity in the field of point-of-care diagnosis of vitamin D deficiency. Currently, we are researching these areas as well as further validating the performance of these sensors and fabricating a reliable and affordable paper-based disposable devices.
